items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.
The answer examiners want is: Rapid prototyping. “Forging” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's Working prototype. “Sketch” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Number of common modelling stages typically taught (sketch, block, working)?
items.why: The real figure is 3 stages. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
